speaker: interviewer
text: --want me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling. Just-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- this is tape number four with survivor Catheryne Morgan on August 15, 1995, in New York, New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Eugene Ilkovic. And he was an officer in-- during World War I. So I assume that this must be-- have been taken between 1914 and 1918 somewhere in the great Austro-Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kingdom, or whatever. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Empire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Empire. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of the three of us. We spent summers together. In the middle is my sister Eva. To the left is George, my cousin. And I'm to the right. We spent summers always at my grandparents' beautiful villa. And here we are with a bowl of cherries. And all I remember that after the picture was taken, we grabbed the cherries and fought over each one of them. [LAUGHS] We sat for this. It's a formal portrait. The three of us, again. It was very special, because the Hertz's came to Paris, and then eventually to Vrutky. And they brought us from Paris these beautiful pink silk dresses. And we took this photograph so we can give it to them as a memento of their visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, about how old were you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- let's see, this is in Vrutky. I must-- probably about five. It was preschool, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you and your sister dressed alike?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always dressed alike, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture came to us in Rome. Our family, fortunately, we evidently were sending them always pictures. Or my cousin must have taken some of them with him, George, who spend the war years there. And after the war, they were nice enough to return all these pictures to us. And some of it, I happened to find a photographer from Budapest, and she had some negatives in her studio. And she made some copies for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's 1944. That's the last picture of my mother and her sister, Aunt Rose, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- you mother's on which side? The left side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is to the left. Aunt Rose to the right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1942 or '43. I'm performing in a school play in the Czech national costume at a dance. In 1943, our father took us to Budapest for a few weeks. And we sat for an official portrait, both my sister and myself. And I found the photographer, by chance, in Rome. And she happened to remember that she has some negatives in her studio. And subsequently, she mailed me the copies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my sister, Eva. This is Italy, 1949, just before I left for Canada. That's my passport picture. And here I'm wearing the lovely coral necklace that my aunt bought on the beaches of Austria for me. [INAUDIBLE]. That's a honeymoon picture, 1954, January. We honeymooned in Miami. And a friend of mine has snapped this. As a matter of fact, they didn't even know it's in existence. She has sent it to us just fairly recently as a matter of fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were 28 when you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of Aunt Rose. It was taken in Italy. And of course, I treasure it as her official portrait. [LAUGHS] Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the affairs that we attended through Fred's business. I don't know whether this is the one Fred-- when we moved to Switzerland, Fred got a beautiful bon voyage party from the firm. And it-- this could be one of-- one of the pictures from there. I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very special grave. It's the gave of my grandfather. And that's the one, I believe, I mentioned in our interview where my aunt went to Auschwitz, took some earth, and made this plaque for our martyred family, being that we truly don't have a grave for them, or do we know of any in existence. And so this is the only memorial we have for our martyred family. And Fred and I see to it that it's kept, and we take care of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I received the prayer book in [INAUDIBLE] in 1945, I believe it was, when we got there from an American soldier. It belong-- of course, it's a Army issue. And this prayer book has been with me in all these many years, in good times and bad, and needless to say is my treasured possession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: Catheryne, will you introduce your husband to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly, my pleasure. This is my husband Fred. We were married January 3, 1954. And been living happily ever after, truly. He's a wonderful guy. And his family has adopted me-- a small but lovely family. And I truly feel I'm part of them. And Fred is a great guy, very supportive in every way. Although he himself went through some hardships, thank goodness the family was able to leave Vienna in 1939. And I still tease him that he's a jailbird. He wouldn't tell me why he was jailed in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fred,can you just tell us what it means to you today, your wife tell her story?

speaker: interviewee
text: What? I didn't get you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, how do you feel today, your wife telling her story to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it-- it-- it-- it gets me always. I get always very riled up. And I get-- it's not good for me. I have my own story. That's why I'm not too crazy to tell it. But when I hear hers, it's worse than mine and I-- I-- I get very excited.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you are glad that you told-- I told it for--

speaker: interviewer
text: She's a very courageous woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she-- she went through a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You both have a lot of strength.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the stronger one of the two of us. Fred gets very emotional. And while I do, too, I-- it doesn't affect me quite as bad. But he knew I wanted to do it, and he told me to go ahead with it since it was important for me. And I'm ever so grateful that you people can, indeed, record it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gives a better picture, though, than I know. A prettier picture. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we thank you both very much for your time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you. We thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I'm truly grateful for this opportunity. And many thanks for your patience.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
